threats.title: Complete Protocol Takeover via Broken Access Control Modifiers
threats.hypothesis_description: The onlyOwner and onlyManager modifiers in Security.sol evaluate boolean expressions instead of reverting. This allows any external caller to execute all privileged functions, including changing fee destinations, fee percentages, and critical contract references.
threats.investigation_rationale: If access control is non-functional, an attacker can redirect all protocol revenue to themselves by setting the protocol fee to 100% and the destination to their own address, effectively draining all incoming ETH from future buys.
threats.config_evidence: Security.sol lines 10-18 show modifiers without require() or revert() statements.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Modifiers evaluate boolean expressions (msg.sender == owner;) without require/revert

preconditions: Contract deployed with current Security.sol implementation

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker calls setProtocolFeePercent to route all buy ETH to themselves
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker captures 100% of the ETH spent on all subsequent token buys across the protocol

economic_questions: Can the attacker set fees > 100% to drain user funds or break sells?
Can the attacker replace the ERC20 factory to deploy malicious tokens?
validation_requirements: Verify that calls to onlyOwner functions succeed from non-owner addresses
Test the economic impact of setting protocol fee to maximum

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: 100% of all future protocol buy volume

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Immediately after deployment or during high volume

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Protocol treasury and all future buyers
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: 100% loss of buy funds to attacker

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Fee routing logic after parameter change

threats.title: FeeSplitter Drain via Unprotected setCurves Function
threats.hypothesis_description: The setCurves function in FeeSplitter.sol lacks any access control modifier. Any user can point the FeeSplitter to a malicious contract that returns fabricated balances, allowing them to claim all ETH held in the FeeSplitter.
threats.investigation_rationale: FeeSplitter relies entirely on the Curves contract for balanceOf and totalSupply data to calculate fee entitlements. Controlling this reference allows arbitrary fee extraction.
threats.config_evidence: FeeSplitter.sol lines 35-37 show setCurves is public with no modifiers.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Missing access control modifier on critical configuration setter

preconditions: FeeSplitter contract holds accumulated ETH fees

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker sets malicious Curves contract returning massive balance for themselves
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker claims 100% of the ETH balance in the FeeSplitter

economic_questions: What is the maximum amount that can be drained in a single transaction?
Does the malicious contract need to simulate specific totalSupply values?
validation_requirements: Deploy mock Curves contract returning specific balances
Call setCurves and then claimFees to verify extraction

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Cost to deploy mock contract (~0.05 ETH)

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When FeeSplitter has accumulated significant unclaimed fees
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Complete loss of all holder fees

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All token holders with unclaimed fees
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Total ETH balance of FeeSplitter contract

threats.title: FeeSplitter Drain via Internal Token Transfers
threats.hypothesis_description: The _transfer function in Curves.sol does not call feeRedistributor.onBalanceChange. When tokens are transferred to a new address, the recipient's userFeeOffset remains 0, allowing them to instantly claim fees calculated from the beginning of the token's history.
threats.investigation_rationale: Fee accounting relies on userFeeOffset being updated whenever balances change. Bypassing this update allows a user to claim fees they did not earn, draining the contract.
threats.config_evidence: Curves.sol lines 272-284 show _transfer updates balances but omits FeeSplitter notification.

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Transferring tokens to a fresh address with 0 offset
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker extracts historical fees they did not earn, draining the FeeSplitter

economic_questions: Can the attacker loop transfers between multiple fresh addresses to drain the entire contract?
Does the sender's stale offset also allow double-claiming?
validation_requirements: Test transfer to fresh address and subsequent claimFees
Calculate exact extraction amount per loop

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: High, limited only by FeeSplitter balance

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After significant trading volume has generated high cumulative fees

threats.title: Retroactive Fee Extraction via Withdraw/Deposit Cycle
threats.hypothesis_description: The withdraw and deposit functions modify internal token balances but fail to update the user's fee offset in the FeeSplitter. A user can withdraw to ERC20, wait for fees to accumulate at a higher per-token rate (due to lower internal supply), deposit back, and retroactively claim fees at the inflated rate for the period they didn't hold internal tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: This desynchronization between internal balances and fee offsets allows users to game the fee distribution mechanism, extracting disproportionate yield without maintaining their internal stake.
threats.config_evidence: Curves.sol lines 465-496 show withdraw/deposit logic without FeeSplitter notification.

proof_of_signal.pattern: withdraw and deposit modify internal balances without updating FeeSplitter offsets

preconditions: Trading volume occurs while attacker holds ERC20 instead of internal tokens

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: FeeSplitter.totalSupply excludes locked tokens; cumulativeFeePerToken increases faster
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker's offset remains stale while they hold ERC20, then they deposit and claim at full balance
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker captures fees distributed to other holders during their absence, at an artificially inflated rate

economic_questions: How much does the cumulativeFeePerToken inflate when a large holder withdraws?
What is the exact profit margin of this cycle compared to holding internally?
validation_requirements: Simulate withdraw -> trade volume -> deposit -> claim sequence
Compare extracted fees vs legitimate holding fees

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas for withdraw, deposit, and claim
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Variable based on trading volume and withdrawn supply
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Multi-block (requires trading volume between withdraw and deposit)

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Dilution of legitimate internal token holders

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Significant portion of holder fee pool

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Impact of locked tokens on cumulative fee rate

threats.title: Destruction of Unclaimed Yield via onBalanceChange
threats.hypothesis_description: The onBalanceChange function in FeeSplitter overwrites a user's userFeeOffset with the current cumulativeFeePerToken without first crediting their accrued fees to unclaimedFees. Any buy or sell action permanently erases the user's accumulated yield.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a critical accounting flaw that destroys user value during normal protocol operation. Furthermore, since onlyManager is broken, an attacker can call this function to maliciously wipe any user's fees.
threats.config_evidence: FeeSplitter.sol lines 80-84 show offset overwrite without updateFeeCredit call.

proof_of_signal.pattern: onBalanceChange overwrites userFeeOffset without calling updateFeeCredit first

preconditions: User buys/sells tokens OR attacker calls onBalanceChange directly

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Normal trading or direct malicious call wipes pending fees
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit for attacker, but massive economic loss for victims (griefing)

economic_questions: Does the wiped ETH remain permanently locked in the FeeSplitter?
Can this be weaponized to increase the relative share of the attacker?
validation_requirements: Test fee accumulation followed by a buy transaction, verify fees drop to 0

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When victims have large unclaimed fee balances
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Loss of user trust and trapped ETH

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All active traders and targeted users

threats.title: Protocol Revenue Loss due to Locked Sell Fees
threats.hypothesis_description: During a sell transaction, the protocol fee is deducted from the seller's payout but is never transferred to the protocolFeeDestination. The ETH remains permanently locked in the Curves contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: This represents a 100% loss of protocol revenue on the sell side, trapping significant ETH in the contract over time with no recovery mechanism.
threats.config_evidence: Curves.sol lines 236-239 show firstDestination is msg.sender for sells, and protocolFee is not routed elsewhere.

proof_of_signal.pattern: sellValue deducts protocolFee but firstDestination (msg.sender) only receives sellValue; protocolFee is never sent

initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Protocol treasury loses expected revenue; ETH is burned/trapped

economic_questions: Is there any hidden mechanism to sweep the Curves contract balance?
How much ETH is expected to be trapped based on projected volume?
validation_requirements: Execute sell transaction and check Curves contract ETH balance increase

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Accounting of all fees during sell

threats.title: Seller Value Destruction via Locked Unset Referral Fees
threats.hypothesis_description: If a token subject has not set a referral destination, the referral fee is still deducted from the seller's payout during a sell transaction, but the transfer is skipped. This ETH becomes permanently locked in the contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: Sellers are penalized with a fee that goes nowhere, destroying user value and trapping ETH in the contract. This breaks the economic expectation that unset fees should either revert to the user or default to the protocol.
threats.config_evidence: Curves.sol lines 245-250 show referral transfer is skipped if undefined, but fee was already deducted.

proof_of_signal.pattern: If referralDefined is false, referralFee is deducted from sellValue but not transferred

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: sellValue deducts referralFee; call is skipped via (true, bytes(''))
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Normal protocol usage for tokens without referrals traps ETH
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Sellers lose value; ETH is burned/trapped

economic_questions: What percentage of tokens are expected to launch without referrals?
Does this create an incentive for subjects to set themselves as the referral?
validation_requirements: Execute sell transaction on token without referral and check Curves contract ETH balance

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Loss of referralFee percentage on every sell
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All referral fees on sells for unset tokens

threats.title: Value Loss from Unrefunded Buy Overpayments
threats.hypothesis_description: The _buyCurvesToken function requires msg.value to be greater than or equal to the price plus fees, but it does not refund any excess ETH sent by the user. The overpayment is permanently locked in the contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: Users interacting via smart contract wallets, aggregators, or UIs that pad gas/value estimates will permanently lose funds. This breaks standard payable function safety patterns.
threats.config_evidence: Curves.sol line 211 shows insufficient payment check without refund logic.

proof_of_signal.pattern: msg.value < price + totalFee reverts, but excess is not refunded

initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Users lose excess ETH; ETH is burned/trapped

economic_questions: How often do users overpay in similar bonding curve protocols?
Can an attacker force a user to overpay (e.g., via frontrunning price changes)? Note: Frontrunning is out of scope for this threat model, but slippage is still relevant.
validation_requirements: Send excess ETH in buy transaction and verify it remains in contract

threats.title: Fee Claim DoS via userTokens Array Bloat
threats.hypothesis_description: The onBalanceChange function pushes the token address to the userTokens array every time a user's balance changes, without checking for duplicates. An attacker can repeatedly buy 0 tokens to bloat a victim's array (if cross-user calls are possible) or their own array, causing out-of-gas errors during fee claims.
threats.investigation_rationale: Because buying 0 tokens is mathematically allowed (price is 0) and onlyManager is broken, an attacker can freely call onBalanceChange for any user, bloating their array and permanently locking their ability to claim fees via batchClaiming or view their portfolio via getUserTokensAndClaimable.
threats.config_evidence: FeeSplitter.sol line 83 shows array push without duplicate check.

proof_of_signal.pattern: userTokens[account].push(token) called on every balance change without duplicate check

preconditions: Attacker targets a user with significant claimable fees

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Unbounded array growth on public function
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Victim is locked out of claiming fees due to block gas limit

economic_questions: How many iterations are required to hit the block gas limit?
What is the cost to execute this griefing attack?
validation_requirements: Call onBalanceChange 1000 times for a user and attempt batchClaiming

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Before victim attempts to claim large fees

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Inability to use batch claim or UI functions

threats.title: Token Identity Hijacking via Unauthorized ERC20 Deployment
threats.hypothesis_description: The withdraw function automatically deploys the ERC20 representation of a curve if it doesn't exist. It does not check if the caller is the token subject. An attacker can buy 1 token of a new curve and immediately withdraw, forcing the ERC20 to deploy with default metadata (DEFAULT_NAME/SYMBOL).
threats.investigation_rationale: This bypasses the subject's right to set their token's name and symbol via setNameAndSymbol or mint. Once deployed, the metadata is immutable, permanently griefing the creator's brand identity.
threats.config_evidence: Curves.sol lines 468-482 show withdraw triggering _deployERC20 without onlyTokenSubject.
preconditions: Subject has created a curve but not yet called mint() or setNameAndSymbol()

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Bypass of onlyTokenSubject for ERC20 deployment
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker front-runs or quickly follows curve creation with a buy+withdraw
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Extortion of creators who want specific ERC20 branding, or pure griefing

economic_questions: Does the default naming scheme append a counter correctly to prevent collisions?
Can this be used to squat desirable token symbols?
validation_requirements: Buy token as non-subject, call withdraw, verify ERC20 is deployed with default name

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Immediately after a high-profile subject creates their curve
